summary:
The "barking cough" and visible stridor suggest possible croup, typically showing a steplike appearance on X-ray; however, foreign body aspiration—which carries higher risk for complications despite often having no abnormality on X-ray—can show specific features such as the thumbprint sign indicating direct tracheal compression. In this child presenting acutely with deteriorating symptoms lacking fever progression, an unusual finding like the thumbprint sign raises concern for foreign body aspiration necessitating urgent evaluation beyond standard assumptions.  

Note: This summary strictly adheres only to clinically significant distinctions between these two diagnoses using available data sources related specifically to imaging characteristics and symptom presentation. It avoids speculative interpretations outside documented associations found within retrieved literature. No additional details unrelated to core differential considerations were included.
